title: Spinning lace-weight singles on the wheel
subtitle: Targeting 30+ WPI singles in fine merino or Bluefaced Leicester for lace projects
excerpt: Lace-weight singles require a higher twist ratio and a lighter, more controlled hand than DK or aran spinning. The fibre must be fine-grade (merino 19 micron or Bluefaced Leicester top), the wheel set to a high flyer ratio, and the drafting speed slower per inch than for thicker yarn. A 30-WPI lace single is achievable on a standard wheel with the right ratio whorl; finer (40+ WPI) requires either a supported spindle or a wheel specifically designed for lace.

glossaryTerms.definition: The finest standard yarn weight category: approximately 30 to 40 WPI, 400 to 800 metres per 100 g. Used for fine shawls, lace scarves, and doily-weight knitting and crochet.
The mechanical ratio between the drive wheel rotation and the flyer rotation. A higher ratio (e.g. 14:1 or higher) inserts more twist per treadle stroke — needed for fine yarn.

content.text: Lace-weight spinning is controlled, slow work. The goal is
singles at 30 to 40 WPI, thin enough that a hand-knitted stitch is transparent when the finished fabric is held to the light. The wheel's
must be set high; most wheels include a lace or high-speed whorl for this purpose.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing back from the high-speed flyer. A lace whorl runs the flyer significantly faster than standard ratios.

items.name: Fine merino top (19 micron) or BFL top (Bluefaced Leicester)
Spinning wheel with high-ratio whorl (14:1 or higher)

content.text: Step 1: Set the wheel for lace

Fit the high-ratio whorl or change the drive-band position to the lace setting. Set take-up tension to the lightest possible: lace singles are fragile and will break if the bobbin takes them up too fast. A near-zero tension is typical for the first few rows.

Pull out a length of top to about one-third its original thickness before attaching it to the wheel. This pre-drafting reduces the drafting demand at the wheel and makes it easier to maintain even thickness. The top should be almost translucent when held to the light.
Step 3: Spin with worsted draw at slow speed

Use a controlled worsted draw. Treadle at a pace that inserts plenty of twist before the front hand releases. The singles should appear slightly over-twisted on the wheel (spiralling back on themselves if allowed to hang free), and this twist relaxes when the skein is set.

Every 5 m or so, stop and wrap a section of singles around a 1-inch ruler section. Count wraps. Target: 30 to 35 WPI for a standard lace weight. Adjust drafting width if the count is drifting above or below target.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two common problems when spinning lace-weight singles.

items.symptom: Singles break repeatedly at the orifice
items.cause: Take-up tension too high, or the singles are under-twisted for the tension being applied.
items.fix: Reduce take-up to the minimum. Increase twist by treadling faster relative to drafting speed. Lace singles need more twist than thicker yarn to be stable.

items.symptom: Finished skein is over-twisted and coils into a mess when plied or washed
items.cause: Twist angle is high throughout, and the set didn't fully relax the yarn.
items.fix: For lace singles that will be used as singles (not plied), set the twist by soaking the skein and hanging it with a gentle weight at the bottom for 24 hours. More of the spinning twist will relax than for a 2-ply yarn.
